channel.title: Governance on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Governance on emsenn.net

item.title: First Officer
item.description:
A first officer is the office responsible for translating command intent into operational execution. The first officer is second in the ship’s hierarchy: subordinate to the captain, superior to all other officers and crew. The captain sets direction; the first officer makes it happen.

What the first officer does

The first officer holds three functions that together bridge command and execution:

  1. Watch management — maintaining continuous awareness of the ship’s operational state. What work is in progress, what is blocked, what has changed, what needs attention. In naval practice, the executive officer (XO) is responsible for the ship’s internal administration and the conduct of the crew (US Navy Regulations, Chapter 10). The watch function is not passive observation — it is active maintenance of the conditions under which work can proceed.

Appeal

Formal definition

An Appeal is a quadruple P=(J1,J2,ϕ,λ)\mathcal{P} = (J_1, J_2, \phi, \lambda):

P=(J1:Jurisdiction,  J2:Jurisdiction,  ϕ∈Ht∗∗(n),  λ:Standing)\mathcal{P} = (J_1 : \mathrm{Jurisdiction},\; J_2 : \mathrm{Jurisdiction},\; \phi \in H^{*(n)}_{t^*},\; \lambda : \mathrm{Standing})

where:

  • J1J_1 is the originating jurisdiction — the forum that issued the judgment ϕ\phi; J1J_1 operates at depth nn in the filtration tower; its fiber Ht∗(n)H^{(n)}_{t^*} is the domain from which the appeal originates
  • J2J_2 is the appellate jurisdiction — the forum with authority to review ϕ\phi; J2J_2 operates at depth k≥nk \geq n; its fiber Ht∗(k)H^{(k)}_{t^*} is richer, containing the full depth-nn context plus additional normative distinctions
  • ϕ∈Ht∗∗(n)\phi \in H^{*(n)}_{t^*} is the judgment being appealed — a proposition that is σ\sigma-settled at depth nn (meaning-settled by J1J_1’s nucleus) but whose Δ\Delta-status at depth kk is not yet determined
  • λ\lambda is the appeal standing — the appellant’s Hohfeldian privilege plus correlative claim-recognition in J2J_2; without λ\lambda, J2J_2’s σ\sigma-nucleus does not process the appellant’s submissions

The appeal morphism is the composition:

Assignment

Formal definition

An Assignment is a triple A=(Q,C,B)\mathcal{A} = (Q, C, B):

A=(Q:Assignor,  C∈Ht∗∗(Q),  B:Assignee)\mathcal{A} = (Q : \mathrm{Assignor},\; C \in H^*_{t^*}(Q),\; B : \mathrm{Assignee})

together with an assignment act α:Ht∗∗(Q)→Ht∗∗(B)\alpha : H^*_{t^*}(Q) \to H^*_{t^*}(B):

α(C)=C′∈Ht∗∗(B)\alpha(C) = C' \in H^*_{t^*}(B)

where C′C' is the transferred claim — the same normative relationship (right to performance by the obligor PP) now held by BB rather than QQ.

  • QQ is the assignor — the original holder of CC; after α\alpha, QQ no longer holds CC
  • C∈Ht∗∗(Q)C \in H^*_{t^*}(Q) is the assigned claim — a Hohfeldian claim (right to receive something from PP); the settled right held by QQ that is being transferred to BB
  • BB is the assignee — the party who receives CC; after α\alpha, BB holds the right to demand performance from PP that QQ previously held
  • α\alpha is the assignment act — the notification, writing, or formal act that constitutes the transfer; the assignment is effective against the obligor PP when PP has notice of α\alpha

Three invariants. A\mathcal{A} is a valid assignment iff:

Estoppel

Formal definition

An Estoppel is a quadruple E=(Q,R,ρ,δ)\mathcal{E} = (Q, R, \rho, \delta):

E=(Q:EstoppedParty,  R:RelyingParty,  ρ∈Htρ,  δ∈Htδ)\mathcal{E} = (Q : \mathrm{EstoppedParty},\; R : \mathrm{RelyingParty},\; \rho \in H_{t_\rho},\; \delta \in H_{t_\delta})

where:

  • QQ is the estopped party — the party whose representation or conduct gave rise to the estoppel; after estoppel is established, QQ cannot assert a position inconsistent with ρ\rho in the current forum
  • RR is the relying party — the party who relied on QQ’s representation ρ\rho and changed their position as a result; RR is the equitable beneficiary of the estoppel
  • ρ∈Htρ\rho \in H_{t_\rho} is the representation — the proposition that QQ asserted, represented, or stood by at time tρt_\rho; may be an explicit statement, a course of conduct, or a silence where there was a duty to speak
  • δ∈Htδ\delta \in H_{t_\delta} is the detriment — the harm that RR would suffer if QQ were now permitted to assert a position inconsistent with ρ\rho; the detriment must be real and caused by RR’s reliance on ρ\rho

The estoppel condition: the forum JJ’s σ-nucleus refuses to settle QQ’s inconsistent claim CC while the estoppel holds. In formal terms: the forum’s site topology JJ does not contain any covering sieve S∈J(t)S \in J(t) that would make CC locally valid — the local sieve generated by QQ’s inconsistent claim fails to cover, because JJ treats the combination of ρ\rho and CC as incompatible.

Novation

Formal definition

A Novation is a triple N=(K1,K2,π)\mathcal{N} = (K_1, K_2, \pi):

N=(K1:Obligation,  K2:Obligation,  π:Agreement(K1,K2))\mathcal{N} = (K_1 : \mathrm{Obligation},\; K_2 : \mathrm{Obligation},\; \pi : \mathrm{Agreement}(K_1, K_2))

together with:

  • An extinction act ε:K1→∅\varepsilon : K_1 \to \emptyset — the termination of the original obligation; K1K_1’s obligations, rights, and claims are discharged in full at the moment π\pi is effective
  • A genesis act γ:∅→K2\gamma : \emptyset \to K_2 — the constitution of the replacement obligation; K2K_2 comes into existence simultaneously with K1K_1’s extinction
  • The agreement π\pi — the consent of all parties whose rights are extinguished or created by the novation; π\pi must be given by every party whose position changes materially

Four invariants. N\mathcal{N} is a valid novation iff:

Hohfeldian Position

What this is

A HohfeldianPosition is one of the eight fundamental legal concepts identified by Wesley Newcomb Hohfeld (Fundamental Legal Conceptions as Applied in Judicial Reasoning, Yale Law Journal, 1913–1917). The insight: the word “right” is used to mean at least four distinct things; juridical confusion follows from failing to distinguish them. Hohfeld’s analysis provides the primitive vocabulary that makes normative relations precisely statable.

The eight concepts form two quartets. Each quartet has an internal structure of opposites (what you don’t have if you have this) and correlatives (what the other party has, necessarily, when you have this).

Ratification

Formal definition

A Ratification is a triple Q=(O,a,ϱ)\mathcal{Q} = (O, a, \varrho):

Q=(O:Office,  a:Act,  ϱ:Declaration)\mathcal{Q} = (O : \mathrm{Office},\; a : \mathrm{Act},\; \varrho : \mathrm{Declaration})

where:

  • OO is the authorizing office — the office that holds (or has held) the Hohfeldian power to authorize acts of the type performed by aa; OO’s authority over the domain D(a)D(a) is the precondition for valid ratification
  • aa is the unauthorized act — an act performed by an agent pp within OO’s domain D(a)D(a) but without OO’s prior authorization; aa’s propositional content ϕa∈Hta\phi_a \in H_{t_a} was not in Hta∗H^*_{t_a} at the time of performance (σta(ϕa)≠ϕa\sigma_{t_a}(\phi_a) \neq \phi_a)
  • ϱ\varrho is the ratification declaration — a speech act by OO (or by the holder of OO) that settles ϕa\phi_a retrospectively: after ϱ\varrho, σt∗(ϕa)=ϕa\sigma_{t^*}(\phi_a) = \phi_a and the act is treated as authorized from tat_a

The ratification acts as a retrospective σt∗\sigma_{t^*} application: ϕa\phi_a moves from Hta∖Hta∗H_{t_a} \setminus H^*_{t_a} (unsettled at the time of performance) into Ht∗∗H^*_{t^*} (settled as of the ratification moment t∗t^*), and the history is adjusted so that ϕa\phi_a is treated as having been in Hta∗H^*_{t_a}.

Relief

Formal definition

A Relief is a triple R=(O,h,R)\mathfrak{R} = (O, h, R):

R=(O:Office,  h:Ht∗∗,  R:ReasonClass)\mathfrak{R} = (O : \mathrm{Office},\; h : H^*_{t^*},\; R : \mathrm{ReasonClass})

together with a dissolution act ε:Ht∗∗→⊥t∗\varepsilon : H^*_{t^*} \to \bot_{t^*}:

ε(h)=⊥Ht∗\varepsilon(h) = \bot_{H_{t^*}}

where:

  • OO is the office — the position being vacated; OO carries its full Hohfeldian deontic profile (authority, accountability, non-delegable duties) independently of who holds it
  • h∈Ht∗∗h \in H^*_{t^*} is the current holder’s binding — the role-assignment proposition that is currently meaning-settled: σt∗(h)=h\sigma_{t^*}(h) = h and Δt∗(h)=h\Delta_{t^*}(h) = h; the holder is in the doubly-quiescent layer
  • RR is the reason class — one of the structurally distinct cases (see below); the reason determines whether mutual acknowledgement μ\mu is required
  • ε\varepsilon is the dissolution act — the formal event that drives hh from Ht∗∗H^*_{t^*} to ⊥Ht∗\bot_{H_{t^*}}: the binding is dissolved, the section sO↦hs_O \mapsto h is replaced by sO↦⊥s_O \mapsto \bot, and the office is vacant

Three invariants. R\mathfrak{R} is a valid relief iff:

Revocation

Formal definition

A Revocation is a triple R=(Q,G,B)\mathcal{R} = (Q, G, B):

R=(Q:Principal,  G⊆Authority(Q),  B:Beneficiary)\mathcal{R} = (Q : \mathrm{Principal},\; G \subseteq \mathrm{Authority}(Q),\; B : \mathrm{Beneficiary})

together with a retraction act ρ:HG→⊥HQ\rho : H_G \to \bot_{H_Q}:

ρ:HG↪HQ  →  ⊥HQ\rho : H_G \hookrightarrow H_Q \;\to\; \bot_{H_Q}

where:

  • QQ is the revoking principal — the original grantor of GG; the party who holds the authority GG as part of their operative domain Authority(Q)\mathrm{Authority}(Q) and who retains the Hohfeldian power to withdraw it
  • GG is the grant being revoked — the sub-domain of QQ’s authority that was delegated to BB; GG is a subset G⊆Authority(Q)G \subseteq \mathrm{Authority}(Q) that was shared as a sub-presheaf HG↪HQH_G \hookrightarrow H_Q
  • BB is the beneficiary — the agent from whose operative domain GG is being withdrawn; after ρ\rho, BB no longer has authority within GG
  • ρ\rho is the retraction act — the formal event (written order, notification, explicit withdrawal) that collapses BB’s access to GG back to ⊥HQ\bot_{H_Q}: the sub-presheaf HGH_G is retracted from BB’s domain

Three invariants. R\mathcal{R} is a valid revocation iff:

Succession

Formal definition

A Succession is a pair S=(O,L)\mathcal{S} = (O, L):

S=(O:Office,  L=(h0,h1,…,hn):PriorityList)\mathcal{S} = (O : \mathrm{Office},\; L = (h_0, h_1, \ldots, h_n) : \mathrm{PriorityList})

together with:

  • An activation predicate P:Ht∗→{0,1}P : H_{t^*} \to \{0, 1\} — the condition under which the primary holder h0h_0 is no longer able to maintain the settled binding h0∈Ht∗∗h_0 \in H^*_{t^*}
  • An availability predicate Ai:Ht∗→{0,1}A_i : H_{t^*} \to \{0, 1\} for each candidate hih_i — whether hih_i is present, capable, and eligible to assume OO
  • A succession act ιi\iota_i — the emergency investiture of the minimum-index available candidate hih_i when PP fires

The succession selects the active section hi∗h_{i^*} where:

Waiver

Formal definition

A Waiver is a triple W=(Q,C,ω)\mathcal{W} = (Q, C, \omega):

W=(Q:RightHolder,  C∈Ht∗∗,  ω:Ht∗∗→⊥t∗)\mathcal{W} = (Q : \mathrm{RightHolder},\; C \in H^*_{t^*},\; \omega : H^*_{t^*} \to \bot_{t^*})

where:

  • QQ is the right-holder — the party whose Hohfeldian claim or immunity CC is being relinquished; QQ must be the current holder of CC in Ht∗∗H^*_{t^*}
  • C∈Ht∗∗C \in H^*_{t^*} is the claim or immunity — the Hohfeldian position being waived; it is doubly settled: σt∗(C)=C\sigma_{t^*}(C) = C and Δt∗(C)=C\Delta_{t^*}(C) = C; the right exists and has transferred into the settled layer
  • ω\omega is the waiver act — the formal event (written release, explicit verbal relinquishment, or course of conduct clearly manifesting intent to waive) that drives CC from Ht∗∗H^*_{t^*} to ⊥t∗\bot_{t^*}; after ω\omega, CC is extinguished and the correlative duty on the obligor lapses

Three invariants. W\mathcal{W} is a valid waiver iff:

Jurisdiction

Formal definition

Jurisdiction is a four-tuple J=(A,TJ,HJ,V)\mathcal{J} = (A, T_J, H_J, V):

J=(A:Authority,  TJ↪T,  HJ↪H∣TJ,  V:TJ×HJ→Ω)\mathcal{J} = (A : \mathrm{Authority},\; T_J \hookrightarrow T,\; H_J \hookrightarrow H|_{T_J},\; V : T_J \times H_J \to \Omega)

where:

  • AA is the authority — the party whose normative powers are operative within the jurisdiction; AA holds second-quartet Hohfeldian Powers within (TJ,HJ)(T_J, H_J) and lacks them outside it
  • TJ⊆TT_J \subseteq T is the history sub-site — the temporal/spatial/subject-matter scope of the jurisdiction; acts at histories t∈TJt \in T_J fall within jurisdiction; acts at t∉TJt \notin T_J do not; TJT_J is equipped with the Grothendieck topology induced from (T,J)(T, J)
  • HJ⊆H∣TJH_J \subseteq H|_{T_J} is the fiber sub-bundle — the sub-fiber of the history fiber restricted to TJT_J that the jurisdiction covers; not all of HtH_t at t∈TJt \in T_J need be within jurisdiction — jurisdiction is typically restricted to a subject-matter sub-fiber (criminal matters, admiralty, equity)
  • V:TJ×HJ→ΩV : T_J \times H_J \to \Omega is the validity condition — the predicate that an act at (t,a)∈TJ×HJ(t, a) \in T_J \times H_J satisfies in order to fall within AA’s jurisdiction; acts that satisfy VV are jurisdictionally valid; those that fail VV are ultra vires

Jurisdiction as sub-topos. J\mathcal{J} corresponds to a sub-topos:

JurisdictionalDeclination

Formal definition

A JurisdictionalDeclination is a triple (A,φ,G)(A, \varphi, G):

(A:Authority,  φ∈HtJ,  G:DeclGround)(A : \mathrm{Authority},\; \varphi \in H^J_t,\; G : \mathrm{DeclGround})

where:

  • AA is the declining authority — the forum that holds valid jurisdiction over φ\varphi; the critical presupposition is that JV(A,φ,t)∈Ht∗J\mathrm{JV}(A, \varphi, t) \in H^{*\mathcal{J}}_t — jurisdiction is established before declination can occur
  • φ∈HtJ\varphi \in H^J_t is the matter — the active matter before AA (introduced by an JurisdictionalInvocation); it is within AA’s sub-fiber
  • GG is the declination ground — the normative basis on which AA declines; ground is required because declination is not a right but a power with conditions; the normative system that constitutes AA specifies which grounds are valid

JurisdictionalDeclination vs. dismissal. These are structurally opposite outcomes following invocation:

JurisdictionalExcess

Formal definition

JurisdictionalExcess is the triple (B,J,φ)(B, \mathcal{J}, \varphi):

(B:Officer,  J=(A,TJ,HJ,V):Jurisdiction,  φ∈Ht)(B : \mathrm{Officer},\; \mathcal{J} = (A, T_J, H_J, V) : \mathrm{Jurisdiction},\; \varphi \in H_t)

where the excess condition holds:

Qvires(φ,A,t)=1  ∧  Qvires(φ,B,t)=0Q_{\mathrm{vires}}(\varphi, A, t) = 1 \;\wedge\; Q_{\mathrm{vires}}(\varphi, B, t) = 0

φ\varphi is intra vires within the full jurisdiction J\mathcal{J} but outside BB’s personal sub-jurisdiction Z=J∣BZ = \mathcal{J}|_B.

Sub-jurisdiction. Z=J∣B=(B,TJ∩TB,HJ∩HB,V∣TB×HB)Z = \mathcal{J}|_B = (B, T_J \cap T_B, H_J \cap H_B, V|_{T_B \times H_B}) is the restriction of J\mathcal{J} to BB’s temporal scope TBT_B and fiber scope HBH_B, from Jurisdiction §Delegation. BB holds second-quartet Hohfeldian Powers within ZZ and lacks them outside it, even within J\mathcal{J}.

JurisdictionalInvocation

Formal definition

An JurisdictionalInvocation is a triple (G,F,φ)(G, F, \varphi):

Inv=(G:Person,  F:Forum,  φ∈Ht)\mathcal{Inv} = (G : \mathrm{Person},\; F : \mathrm{Forum},\; \varphi \in H_t)

where:

  • GG is the invoker — the party introducing the matter; GG must hold assertive Standing in FF for the act-class Φ\Phi that includes introducing matters of the type φ\varphi
  • FF is the forum with jurisdiction J=(A,TJ,HJ,V)\mathcal{J} = (A, T_J, H_J, V) — the normative system whose nucleus σtJ\sigma^{\mathcal{J}}_t is being requested to operate on φ\varphi
  • φ∈Ht\varphi \in H_t is the matter — the fiber element being introduced; it exists in HtH_t prior to invocation but is not in FF’s sub-fiber HtJH^J_t until GG’s act places it there

The invocation act. GG’s invocation is a fiber element δinv∈Htinv\delta_{\mathrm{inv}} \in H^{\mathrm{inv}}_t — an element in the invocation sub-fiber — that satisfies:

JurisdictionalSubmission

Formal definition

A JurisdictionalSubmission is a triple (G,A,S)(G, A, S):

(G:Person,  A:Authority,  S⊆HJ)(G : \mathrm{Person},\; A : \mathrm{Authority},\; S \subseteq H_J)

where:

  • GG is the submitting party — the party who consents to AA’s jurisdiction; GG typically holds immunity from AA’s jurisdiction by default (personal jurisdiction requires consent or presence) and submission is the act that lifts that immunity
  • AA is the authority with jurisdiction J=(A,TJ,HJ,V)\mathcal{J} = (A, T_J, H_J, V) — the forum to which GG is consenting
  • S⊆HJS \subseteq H_J is the matter-scope — the specific sub-fiber within AA’s subject-matter jurisdiction that the submission covers; GG may submit to personal jurisdiction for some matters and not others

The submission act. JurisdictionalSubmission is an act δsub∈Htsub\delta_{\mathrm{sub}} \in H^{\mathrm{sub}}_t that settles the personal jurisdiction component of VV:

JurisdictionalVires

Formal definition

JurisdictionalVires is the quality Qvires:ActClass×Authority×T→{0,1}Q_{\mathrm{vires}} : \mathrm{ActClass} \times \mathrm{Authority} \times T \to \{0, 1\} defined by:

Qvires(φ,A,t)={1if JV(A,φ,t)∈Ht∗J0otherwiseQ_{\mathrm{vires}}(\varphi, A, t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \mathrm{JV}(A, \varphi, t) \in H^{*\mathcal{J}}_t \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}

where JV(A,φ,t)=RJ(pos(A,t),φ,V(t,φ))\mathrm{JV}(A, \varphi, t) = R_{\mathcal{J}}(\mathrm{pos}(A, t), \varphi, V(t, \varphi)) is the jurisdictional validity proposition from Jurisdiction.

Qvires=1Q_{\mathrm{vires}} = 1 is called intra vires (“within the powers”). Qvires=0Q_{\mathrm{vires}} = 0 is called ultra vires (“beyond the powers”).

A ship is a large vessel built to travel on water, carrying people and goods. Boats are small enough for one person to handle. Ships are big enough to need a crew.

A ship has a hull (the physical structure that floats), a deck (the working surface), holds (enclosed spaces below deck for cargo), and some means of propulsion — sails, engines, oars. The hull keeps the water out. Everything else happens inside or on top of it.

A captain is the person in command of a ship, aircraft, or military unit. The captain makes the decisions, bears the responsibility, and answers for what happens.

On a ship, the captain is called the master. The master’s authority comes from being formally assigned to command — not from rank, seniority, or ownership. A ship owner who has not been installed as master has no command authority aboard. A junior officer who has been installed as master has full command authority. Command is a position, not a personal attribute.

A crew is the group of people who operate a ship, aircraft, or vehicle. The crew sails the ship, flies the plane, drives the tank. Passengers ride. Crew work.

The ship determines what the crew must be. A cargo vessel needs navigators, engineers, and deckhands. A warship needs those plus weapons operators, sensor technicians, and damage control teams. A fishing boat needs a skipper and hands. The required roles come from what the ship is and what it does — the crew fills those roles with actual people.
